sourceQuestion:
In the following table, the left column contains the names of standard graph algorithms and the right column contains the time complexities of the algorithms. Match each algorithm with its time complexity.

Algorithms:
1. Bellman-Ford algorithm
2. Kruskal's algorithm
3. Floyd-Warshall algorithm
4. Topological sorting

Time Complexities:
A: O(m log n)
B: O(n^2)
C: O(nm)
D: O(n + m)

Options:
A. 1-C, 2-A, 3-B, 4-D
B. 1-B, 2-D, 3-A, 4-C
C. 1-C, 2-A, 3-B, 4-D
D. 1-B, 2-A, 3-C, 4-D

parsed.stem:
In the following table, the left column contains the names of standard graph algorithms and the right column contains the time complexities of the algorithms. Match each algorithm with its time complexity.

Algorithms:
1. Bellman-Ford algorithm
2. Kruskal's algorithm
3. Floyd-Warshall algorithm
4. Topological sorting

Time Complexities:

sourceAnswer: B. E1: A[i][j] and E2: A[j][i], i != j

options.text: E1: A[i][j] and E2: A[j][i], i = j
E1: A[i][j] and E2: A[j][i], i != j
E1: !A[i][j] and E2: A[j][i], i = j
E1: A[i][j] and E2: !A[j][i], i = j

sourceQuestion:
The most appropriate matching for the following pairs is:

X: depth first search     1: heap
Y: breadth first search   2: queue
Z: sorting                3: stack

A. X-1, Y-2, Z-3
B. X-3, Y-2, Z-1
C. X-3, Y-1, Z-2
D. X-2, Y-3, Z-1

parsed.stem:
The most appropriate matching for the following pairs is:

X: depth first search     1: heap
Y: breadth first search   2: queue
Z: sorting                3: stack

sourceQuestion:
Let G be an undirected graph. Consider a depth-first traversal of G, and let T be the resulting depth-first search tree. Let u be a vertex in G and let v be the first new (unvisited) vertex visited after visiting u in the traversal. Which of the following statements is always true?

A. {u, v} must be an edge in G, and u is a descendant of v in T
B. {u, v} must be an edge in G, and v is a descendant of u in T
C. If {u, v} is not an edge in G, then u is a leaf in T
D. If {u, v} is not an edge in G, then u and v must have the same parent in T
sourceAnswer: C. If {u, v} is not an edge in G, then u is a leaf in T

parsed.stem: Let G be an undirected graph. Consider a depth-first traversal of G, and let T be the resulting depth-first search tree. Let u be a vertex in G and let v be the first new (unvisited) vertex visited after visiting u in the traversal. Which of the following statements is always true?

options.text: {u, v} must be an edge in G, and u is a descendant of v in T
{u, v} must be an edge in G, and v is a descendant of u in T
If {u, v} is not an edge in G, then u is a leaf in T
If {u, v} is not an edge in G, then u and v must have the same parent in T

sourceQuestion:
Consider an undirected, unweighted graph G. Let a breadth-first traversal of G be done starting from a node r. Let d(r, u) and d(r, v) be the lengths of the shortest paths from r to u and v respectively in G. If u is visited before v during the breadth-first traversal, which of the following statements is correct?
A. d(r, u) < d(r, v)
B. d(r, u) > d(r, v)
C. d(r, u) <= d(r, v)
D. None of the above

parsed.stem: Consider an undirected, unweighted graph G. Let a breadth-first traversal of G be done starting from a node r. Let d(r, u) and d(r, v) be the lengths of the shortest paths from r to u and v respectively in G. If u is visited before v during the breadth-first traversal, which of the following statements is correct?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following graph (with vertices a, b, e, f, g, h and edges a-b, b-e, b-f, e-g, e-h, f-h, g-h):

Among the following sequences:
I.   a b e g h f
II.  a b f e h g
III. a b f h e g
IV.  a f g h b e

Which are the depth-first traversals of the above graph?
A. I, II and IV only
B. I and IV only
C. II, III and IV only
D. I, III and IV only
sourceAnswer: A. I, II and IV only

parsed.stem:
Consider the following graph (with vertices a, b, e, f, g, h and edges a-b, b-e, b-f, e-g, e-h, f-h, g-h):

Among the following sequences:
I.   a b e g h f
II.  a b f e h g
III. a b f h e g
IV.  a f g h b e

Which are the depth-first traversals of the above graph?

sourceQuestion:
Let T be a depth first search tree in an undirected graph G. Vertices u and v are leaves of this DFS tree T. The degrees of both u and v in G are at least 2. Which one of the following statements is true?
A. There must exist a vertex w adjacent to both u and v in G
B. There must exist a vertex w whose removal disconnects u and v in G
C. There must exist a cycle in G containing u and v
D. There must exist a cycle in G containing u and all its neighbors in G
sourceAnswer: B. There must exist a vertex w whose removal disconnects u and v in G

parsed.stem: Let T be a depth first search tree in an undirected graph G. Vertices u and v are leaves of this DFS tree T. The degrees of both u and v in G are at least 2. Which one of the following statements is true?

options.text: There must exist a vertex w adjacent to both u and v in G
There must exist a vertex w whose removal disconnects u and v in G
There must exist a cycle in G containing u and v
There must exist a cycle in G containing u and all its neighbors in G

A. MNOPQR
B. NQMPOR
C. QMNPOR
D. QMNPRO

sourceQuestion:
Let G be a graph with n vertices and m edges. What is the tightest upper bound on the running time of Depth First Search on G, when G is represented as an adjacency matrix?
A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(n + m)
C. Theta(n^2)
D. Theta(n^2 + m)

parsed.stem: Let G be a graph with n vertices and m edges. What is the tightest upper bound on the running time of Depth First Search on G, when G is represented as an adjacency matrix?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the tree arcs of a BFS traversal from a source node W in an unweighted, connected, undirected graph. The tree T formed by the tree arcs is a data structure for computing
A. the shortest path between every pair of vertices.
B. the shortest path from W to every vertex in the graph.
C. the shortest paths from W to only those nodes that are leaves of T.
D. the longest path in the graph.
sourceAnswer: B. the shortest path from W to every vertex in the graph.

parsed.stem: Consider the tree arcs of a BFS traversal from a source node W in an unweighted, connected, undirected graph. The tree T formed by the tree arcs is a data structure for computing

options.text: the shortest path between every pair of vertices.
the shortest path from W to every vertex in the graph.
the shortest paths from W to only those nodes that are leaves of T.
the longest path in the graph.

sourceQuestion:
Let G = (V, E) be a simple undirected graph, and s be a particular vertex in it called the source. For v in V, let d(v) denote the shortest distance in G from s to v. A breadth first search (BFS) is performed starting at s. Let T be the resultant BFS tree. If (u, v) is an edge of G that is not in T, then which one of the following CANNOT be the value of d(u) - d(v)?
A. -1
B. 0
C. 1
D. 2

parsed.stem: Let G = (V, E) be a simple undirected graph, and s be a particular vertex in it called the source. For v in V, let d(v) denote the shortest distance in G from s to v. A breadth first search (BFS) is performed starting at s. Let T be the resultant BFS tree. If (u, v) is an edge of G that is not in T, then which one of the following CANNOT be the value of d(u) - d(v)?

sourceQuestion:
The Breadth First Search (BFS) algorithm has been implemented using the queue data structure. Which one of the following is a possible order of visiting the nodes in the graph below?
[Graph: Undirected graph with nodes M, N, O, P, Q, R. Edges: M-N, M-O, N-Q, N-P, O-Q, P-R, Q-R]

A. MNOQPR
B. NQMPOR
C. QMNBOP
D. POQNMR

sourceQuestion:
Let G be a simple undirected graph. Let T_D be a depth first search tree of G. Let T_B be a breadth first search tree of G. Consider the following statements.
I. No edge of G is a cross edge with respect to T_D. (A cross edge in G is between two nodes neither of which is an ancestor of the other in T_D.)
II. For every edge (u, v) of G, if d(u) < d(v), then u is an ancestor of v in T_D, where d(u) and d(v) are depths of u and v in T_B.

Which of the statements above must be true?

A. I only
B. II only
C. Both I and II
D. Neither I nor II

parsed.stem:
Let G be a simple undirected graph. Let T_D be a depth first search tree of G. Let T_B be a breadth first search tree of G. Consider the following statements.
I. No edge of G is a cross edge with respect to T_D. (A cross edge in G is between two nodes neither of which is an ancestor of the other in T_D.)
II. For every edge (u, v) of G, if d(u) < d(v), then u is an ancestor of v in T_D, where d(u) and d(v) are depths of u and v in T_B.

Which of the statements above must be true?

sourceQuestion:
Let G be a directed graph and T a depth first search spanning tree in G that is rooted at a vertex s. Suppose T is also a breadth first search tree in G, rooted at s. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE for every such graph G and tree T?

A. There are no back-edges in G with respect to the tree T
B. There are no cross-edges in G with respect to the tree T
C. There are no forward-edges in G with respect to the tree T
D. The only edges in G are the tree edges of T

parsed.stem: Let G be a directed graph and T a depth first search spanning tree in G that is rooted at a vertex s. Suppose T is also a breadth first search tree in G, rooted at s. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE for every such graph G and tree T?

options.text: There are no back-edges in G with respect to the tree T
There are no cross-edges in G with respect to the tree T
There are no forward-edges in G with respect to the tree T
The only edges in G are the tree edges of T

sourceQuestion:
Consider performing depth-first search (DFS) on an undirected and unweighted graph G starting at vertex s. For any vertex v in G, d_G(s, v) is the length of the shortest path from s to v. Let (u, v) be an edge in G such that d_G(s, u) = d_G(s, v). If the edge (u, v) is explored first in the direction from u to v during the above DFS, then (u, v) becomes a _______ edge.
A. tree  B. back  C. cross  D. gray

parsed.stem: Consider performing depth-first search (DFS) on an undirected and unweighted graph G starting at vertex s. For any vertex v in G, d_G(s, v) is the length of the shortest path from s to v. Let (u, v) be an edge in G such that d_G(s, u) = d_G(s, v). If the edge (u, v) is explored first in the direction from u to v during the above DFS, then (u, v) becomes a _______ edge.

sourceQuestion:
In a depth-first traversal of a graph G with n vertices, R edges are marked as tree edges. The number of connected components in G is:
A. k  B. k+1  C. n-k-1  D. n-k

parsed.stem: In a depth-first traversal of a graph G with n vertices, R edges are marked as tree edges. The number of connected components in G is:

sourceQuestion:
Consider the depth-first-search of an undirected graph with 3 vertices P, Q, and R. Let discovery time d(v) represent the time instant when the vertex v is first visited, and finish time f(v) represent the time instant when the vertex v is last visited. Given that:
d(P) = 5,  f(P) = 12
d(Q) = 6,  f(Q) = 10
d(R) = 14, f(R) = 18
Which one of the following statements is TRUE about the DFS?
A. There is only one connected component.
B. There are two connected components, and P and R are connected.
C. There are two connected components, and Q and R are connected.
D. There are two connected components, and Q and R are connected.
sourceAnswer: B. There are two connected components, and P and R are connected.

parsed.stem:
Consider the depth-first-search of an undirected graph with 3 vertices P, Q, and R. Let discovery time d(v) represent the time instant when the vertex v is first visited, and finish time f(v) represent the time instant when the vertex v is last visited. Given that:
d(P) = 5,  f(P) = 12
d(Q) = 6,  f(Q) = 10
d(R) = 14, f(R) = 18
Which one of the following statements is TRUE about the DFS?

options.text: There is only one connected component.
There are two connected components, and P and R are connected.
There are two connected components, and Q and R are connected.
There are two connected components, and Q and R are connected.

sourceQuestion:
A depth-first search is performed on a directed acyclic graph. Let d[u] denote the time at which vertex u is visited for the first time and f[u] the time at which the DFS call to the vertex u terminates. Which of the following statements is always TRUE for all edges (u, v) in the graph?
A. d[u] < d[v]  B. d[u] < d[v] and f[u] > f[v]  C. f[u] > f[v]  D. d[u] > d[v]
sourceAnswer: B. d[u] < d[v] and f[u] > f[v]

parsed.stem: A depth-first search is performed on a directed acyclic graph. Let d[u] denote the time at which vertex u is visited for the first time and f[u] the time at which the DFS call to the vertex u terminates. Which of the following statements is always TRUE for all edges (u, v) in the graph?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following sequence of nodes for the undirected graph given below (graph with vertices a, b, c, d, e, f, g where a connects to b and d; b connects to e and f; e connects to g; g connects to c; c connects to d; f connects to c):

A Depth First Search (DFS) is started at node a. The nodes are listed in the order they are first visited. Which of the following is/are possible outputs?
1. a b e f g c d
2. a b f e g c d
3. a d c g e b f
4. a d c f b e g

A. 1 and 4 only  B. 2 and 3 only  C. 2, 3 and 4 only  D. 1, 2 and 3 only

parsed.stem:
Consider the following sequence of nodes for the undirected graph given below (graph with vertices a, b, c, d, e, f, g where a connects to b and d; b connects to e and f; e connects to g; g connects to c; c connects to d; f connects to c):

A Depth First Search (DFS) is started at node a. The nodes are listed in the order they are first visited. Which of the following is/are possible outputs?
1. a b e f g c d
2. a b f e g c d
3. a d c g e b f
4. a d c f b e g

sourceQuestion:
The minimum number of record movements required to merge five files A (with 10 records), B (with 20 records), C (with 15 records), D (with 30 records) and E (with 25 records) is:
A. 165
B. 90
C. 75
D. 65

parsed.stem: The minimum number of record movements required to merge five files A (with 10 records), B (with 20 records), C (with 15 records), D (with 30 records) and E (with 25 records) is:

sourceQuestion:
The following are the starting and ending times of activities A, B, C, D, E, F, G and H respectively in chronological order:
s_A, s_B, s_C, s_D, s_F, s_E, s_H, s_G, e_A, e_B, e_D, e_C, e_H, e_E, e_G, e_F
Here, s_X denotes the starting time and e_X denotes the ending time of activity X. We need to schedule the activities in a set of rooms available to us. An activity can be scheduled in a room only if the room is reserved for the activity for its entire duration. What is the minimum number of rooms required?
A. 3
B. 4
C. 5
D. 6

parsed.stem:
The following are the starting and ending times of activities A, B, C, D, E, F, G and H respectively in chronological order:
s_A, s_B, s_C, s_D, s_F, s_E, s_H, s_G, e_A, e_B, e_D, e_C, e_H, e_E, e_G, e_F
Here, s_X denotes the starting time and e_X denotes the ending time of activity X. We need to schedule the activities in a set of rooms available to us. An activity can be scheduled in a room only if the room is reserved for the activity for its entire duration. What is the minimum number of rooms required?

sourceQuestion:
We are given 9 tasks T_1, T_2, ..., T_9. The execution of each task requires one unit of time. We can execute one task at a time. Each task T_i has a profit P_i and a deadline d_i. Profit P_i is earned if the task is completed before the end of the d_i-th unit of time. The profits and deadlines are given below:

Task     : T1  T2  T3  T4  T5  T6  T7  T8  T9
Profit   : 15  10   1   7  20  16  25   2   3
Deadline :  7   2   5   3   4   5   2   7   3

Are all tasks completed in the schedule that gives maximum profit?
A. All tasks are completed
B. T_3 and T_9 are left out
C. T_1 and T_6 are left out
D. T_1 and T_4 are left out
sourceAnswer: B. T_3 and T_9 are left out

parsed.stem:
We are given 9 tasks T_1, T_2, ..., T_9. The execution of each task requires one unit of time. We can execute one task at a time. Each task T_i has a profit P_i and a deadline d_i. Profit P_i is earned if the task is completed before the end of the d_i-th unit of time. The profits and deadlines are given below:

Task     : T1  T2  T3  T4  T5  T6  T7  T8  T9
Profit   : 15  10   1   7  20  16  25   2   3
Deadline :  7   2   5   3   4   5   2   7   3

Are all tasks completed in the schedule that gives maximum profit?

options.text: T_3 and T_9 are left out
T_1 and T_6 are left out
T_1 and T_4 are left out

sourceQuestion:
We are given 9 tasks T_1, T_2, ..., T_9. The execution of each task requires one unit of time. We can execute one task at a time. Each task T_i has a profit P_i and a deadline d_i. Profit P_i is earned if the task is completed before the end of the d_i^th unit of time.

Task     | T_1 | T_2 | T_3 | T_4 | T_5 | T_6 | T_7 | T_8 | T_9

Deadline |   7 |   2 |   5 |   3 |   4 |   5 |   2 |   7 |   3

What is the maximum profit earned?

A. 147
B. 165
C. 167
D. 175

sourceQuestion:
A hash table with ten buckets with one slot per bucket is shown in the following figure. The symbols S1 to S7 are initially entered using a hashing function with linear probing. The maximum number of comparisons needed in searching an item that is not present is:

Bucket | Contents
   0   |   S7
   1   |   S1
   2   |  (empty)
   3   |   S4
   4   |   S2
   5   |   S5
   6   |   S6
   7   |  (empty)
   8   |  (empty)
   9   |   S3

A. 4
B. 5
C. 6
D. 3

sourceQuestion:
Consider a dynamic hashing approach for 64-bit integer keys:
1. There is a main hash table of size 4.
2. The least significant 2 bits of a key is used to index into the main hash table.
3. Initially, the main hash table entries are empty.
4. Thereafter, when more keys are hashed into it, to resolve collisions, the set of all keys corresponding to a main hash table entry is organized as a binary tree that grows on demand.
5. The 3rd least significant bit is used to divide the keys into left and right subtrees.
6. To resolve more collisions, each node of the binary tree is further sub-divided into left and right subtrees based on the 4th least significant bit.
7. A split is done only if it is needed, i.e., only when there is a collision.

Consider the following state of the hash table.
[The hash table has: slot 0b00 -> a binary tree with 5,9,13,7 arranged; slot 0b10 -> contains 10; slot 0b01 and 0b11 are empty]

Which of the following sequences of key insertions can cause the above state of the hash table (assume the keys are in decimal notation)?

A. 5, 9, 4, 13, 10, 7
B. 9, 5, 13, 10, 7, 5, 13
C. 10, 9, 7, 5, 13
D. 5, 9, 13, 6, 14, 10, 14

parsed.stem:
Consider a dynamic hashing approach for 64-bit integer keys:
1. There is a main hash table of size 4.
2. The least significant 2 bits of a key is used to index into the main hash table.
3. Initially, the main hash table entries are empty.
4. Thereafter, when more keys are hashed into it, to resolve collisions, the set of all keys corresponding to a main hash table entry is organized as a binary tree that grows on demand.
5. The 3rd least significant bit is used to divide the keys into left and right subtrees.
6. To resolve more collisions, each node of the binary tree is further sub-divided into left and right subtrees based on the 4th least significant bit.
7. A split is done only if it is needed, i.e., only when there is a collision.

Consider the following state of the hash table.
[The hash table has: slot 0b00 -> a binary tree with 5,9,13,7 arranged; slot 0b10 -> contains 10; slot 0b01 and 0b11 are empty]

Which of the following sequences of key insertions can cause the above state of the hash table (assume the keys are in decimal notation)?

sourceQuestion:
Suppose we are given n keys, n hash table slots, and two simple uniform hash functions h_1 and h_2. Further suppose our hashing scheme uses h_1 for the odd keys and h_2 for the even keys. What is the expected number of keys in a slot?

A. 1/n
B. 1
C. n/2
D. 2/n

parsed.stem: Suppose we are given n keys, n hash table slots, and two simple uniform hash functions h_1 and h_2. Further suppose our hashing scheme uses h_1 for the odd keys and h_2 for the even keys. What is the expected number of keys in a slot?

sourceQuestion:
An algorithm has to store several keys generated by an adversary in a hash table. The adversary is malicious who tries to maximize the number of collisions. Let n be the number of keys, m be the number of slots in the hash table, and n < m. Which one of the following is the best hashing strategy to counteract the adversary?

A. Division method, i.e., use the hash function h(k) = k mod m
B. Multiplication method, i.e., use the hash function h(k) = floor(m * (k*A - floor(k*A))), where A is a carefully chosen constant
C. Universal hashing method
D. If m is a prime number, use Division method. Otherwise, use Multiplication method.

parsed.stem: An algorithm has to store several keys generated by an adversary in a hash table. The adversary is malicious who tries to maximize the number of collisions. Let n be the number of keys, m be the number of slots in the hash table, and n < m. Which one of the following is the best hashing strategy to counteract the adversary?

options.text: Division method, i.e., use the hash function h(k) = k mod m
Multiplication method, i.e., use the hash function h(k) = floor(m * (k*A - floor(k*A))), where A is a carefully chosen constant
If m is a prime number, use Division method. Otherwise, use Multiplication method.

sourceQuestion:
A hash table contains 10 buckets and uses linear probing to resolve collisions. The key values are integers and the hash function used is h(k) = k mod 10. If the values 43, 133, 62, 133, 142 are inserted in the table, in what location would the key value 142 be inserted?

A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 6

parsed.stem: A hash table contains 10 buckets and uses linear probing to resolve collisions. The key values are integers and the hash function used is h(k) = k mod 10. If the values 43, 133, 62, 133, 142 are inserted in the table, in what location would the key value 142 be inserted?

sourceQuestion:
Suppose the letters a, b, c, d, e, f have probabilities 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/32 respectively. Which of the following is the Huffman code for the letters a, b, c, d, e, f?

A. 0, 10, 110, 1110, 11110, 11111
B. 11, 10, 01, 001, 0001, 0000
C. 11, 10, 01, 001, 0001, 0000
D. 0, 10, 110, 1110, 11110, 11111

parsed.stem: Suppose the letters a, b, c, d, e, f have probabilities 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/32 respectively. Which of the following is the Huffman code for the letters a, b, c, d, e, f?
